policy_statement: NAP target: reduce cases of human vector-borne diseases associated with climate change by 10% by 2030 (decadal average).
mechanism_description: Implementing vector surveillance, prevention, and control measures reduces the incidence of climate-associated vector-borne diseases, lowering overall climate-related health risks.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (warming/precipitation shifts → expanded vector habitat/season) → public health action (surveillance + prevention + control) → ↓ human exposure/infection → ↓ climate-related health impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: Public health and environmental health systems implement vector surveillance, source reduction, and vector control (plus related preparedness and response).  
2) Mediating process: Reduced vector abundance, reduced human–vector contact, and interrupted pathogen transmission cycles (e.g., limiting breeding sites, targeted insecticide use, early detection and rapid response to outbreaks).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower incidence and severity of climate-amplified vector-borne diseases, reducing climate-related health risk and vulnerability as climatic conditions become more favorable to vectors.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced morbidity and mortality from climate-sensitive vector-borne diseases by lowering exposure and transmission risk as temperatures and precipitation patterns shift.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on a climate-sensitive hazard (vector range/season and transmission suitability) by reducing exposure and disease transmission, thereby reducing health impacts.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be minor mitigation co-effects (e.g., different choices of chemicals/operations) or resource-efficiency elements, the core intended and largest climate-relevant effect is protecting people from climate-related health risks—an adaptation outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy is framed as general disease control without explicit climate linkage, it can still function as adaptation when vectors are climate-sensitive; however, the categorization here is based on the mechanism explicitly referencing climate-associated vector-borne disease risk. Potential negative side-effects (e.g., insecticide emissions, ecological impacts) do not change the dominant pathway classification.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy sharing this mechanism (surveillance/prevention/control to reduce climate-associated vector-borne disease incidence) is fundamentally reducing vulnerability and impact from a climate-amplified hazard, so Adaptation applies across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., “public health preparedness,” “early warning,” “environmental management”) depends on specific policy design and evidence at the individual policy level and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations may include nature-based or infrastructure components (e.g., wetland/drainage management) or may have small mitigation disbenefits (energy use, chemical production); these are typically secondary to the primary health risk reduction pathway.
key_indicators: “NAP target”; “reduce”; “by 10%”; “by 2030”; “cases of human vector-borne diseases associated with climate change”; “decadal average”
co_benefits: Potential general public health strengthening and reduced non-climate-related vector-borne disease burden.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although achieving the target would typically require enabling actions (surveillance, IVM, outreach), the text itself contains only the quantified target, not the operational instruments—so it is coded as target-setting rather than an incentive program, planning strategy, or infrastructure investment.
